groups.text: It is currently a clear morning in Berlin-Tempelhof with a temperature of 32 degrees.
With the morning weather set, here is the news.

Masayoshi Son is questioning Elon Musk’s vision for orbital data centers. The SoftBank CEO argued at a recent shareholder meeting that building data centers in space will not significantly cut costs and will take too long to implement. Son believes the next few years of the AI battle are far more critical than what might happen a decade from now. Separately, Sina has released an open language model called VibeThinker-3B. Despite having only three billion parameters, it matches models like Kimi K2.5 and DeepSeek V3.2 on coding and math benchmarks, even though those models are up to 333 times larger. The performance comes from multi-stage post-training applied to an Alibaba base model. However, the small model falls well behind on tasks requiring broad factual knowledge. Researchers conclude that while structured logical reasoning compresses well into smaller models, world knowledge still requires massive scale.

Prosecutors in Los Angeles used ChatGPT logs as evidence against Jonathan Rinderknecht, who faced arson charges for starting one of the deadliest wildfires in city history on New Year's Day 2025. Along with iPhone location data and security footage, prosecutors presented logs showing Rinderknecht asking the chatbot why he was always angry and ranting about how the wealthy were destroying the world. They also cited a screen recording where he asked if someone could be blamed for a fire lit by their device. Separately, author Margaret Atwood expressed skepticism about the reliability of these tools during an interview at the Babell Literary and Cultural Festival in Portugal. After using Anthropic's Claude to find information on the series Father Brown, Atwood said the AI gave her the wrong answer or lied. She described the problem with artificial intelligence as garbage in, garbage out.

Wall Street investors are searching for the next Nvidia, and they have placed a massive bet on Micron. The Boise-based memory chip maker has seen its stock soar over 236 percent in the last month alone, closing Friday at 1,132 dollars a share. This surge briefly pushed Micron's market valuation above both Meta and Tesla, ending the week with a market cap near 1.27 trillion dollars. The growth is driven by an AI data center boom that has created a severe supply crunch for system memory chips, specifically High-Bandwidth Memory. Hyperscalers like Microsoft, Amazon AWS, Google, Meta, and Oracle are buying these components in large quantities to build AI servers, which require far more memory than standard laptops. While Micron claims it has shored up its long-term position, the company's continued success depends on how long this AI-driven shortage lasts.

Western AI labs are facing a pricing stress test as companies seek cheaper alternatives to OpenAI and Anthropic. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong has moved his company to Chinese models like Kimi 2.7 and GLM 5.2, cutting AI spending in half while increasing token usage. Coinbase now uses an automated routing system to pick the best model based on price and task, while Snowflake is also testing Chinese models for cost savings. Separately, Apple is raising prices across its hardware line, with the 16-inch MacBook Pro increasing by 300 dollars and the HomePod Mini rising to 129 dollars. CEO Tim Cook called these hikes unavoidable, blaming the AI industry's costs. Amidst this shift, Paul Meade, the Apple vice president who led Vision Pro and upcoming smart glasses, is reportedly leaving for OpenAI’s hardware team. This move follows a shake-up in Apple's engineering team under John Ternus.

AI systems are not yet reliable coworkers because they primarily generate answers rather than finishing entire tasks. A survey paper from Tencent’s Youtu Lab and several Chinese universities argues that AI must move beyond reactive question-and-answer sessions to become true digital colleagues. This shift requires models to operate in persistent work environments where they can turn intent into finished work through reusable skills. The researchers map this evolution across five stages, moving from basic chatbots toward autonomous execution. While the chatbot era focused on fast text generation based on language patterns, a new era of thinking LLMs, initiated by Deepseek-R1 and OpenAI's o1, invests more compute during inference. These newer models explore solution paths and verify intermediate steps to correct errors before providing a final answer. The central challenge remains moving from simply answering a prompt to the delegated execution of complex work.

Most current AI models struggle with long-term strategic planning, as shown in a new survival test from Princeton University. Researchers created CEO-Bench to see if AI agents could run a fictional software company for 500 simulated days. The results were stark: only three models finished the simulation with more than their starting capital. Most of the agents went broke during the process. In fact, a simple rule-based heuristic that uses no AI at all beat nearly every model tested. While AI is improving at narrow tasks like fixing bugs or following service policies, it fails at long chains of decisions under uncertainty. The researchers argue that strategic steering intelligence remains fundamentally different from current capabilities. They point to Steve Jobs' 1997 decision to limit Apple's product line as an example of the kind of high-level resource allocation and priority setting that AI cannot yet replicate.

Zhou Hongyi, founder of the Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 Security Technology, is framing the race for AI vulnerability tools as a matter of cyber-nuclear deterrence. Zhou warns that China cannot allow the U.S. to hold a monopoly on these capabilities, comparing advanced models like Anthropic's Mythos to weapons of mass destruction. To close a gap he estimates at 20 to 30 percent between Chinese and Western systems, his company has unveiled two tools: Yi Tian Zhen for automated defense and Tu Long Feng for hunting vulnerabilities. The latter has already flagged over three thousand vulnerabilities. Separately, the personal application of AI is appearing in healthcare. Connor Christou, a founder diagnosed with a rare and aggressive form of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma, used Claude to manage his battle. Christou fed the AI his blood results, scan data, wearable output, and journal entries to help navigate a disease caused by a random genetic mutation.
After the headlines, back to the weather.
This afternoon will bring rain with temperatures between 20 and 29 degrees.
And from the weather, a quick look at the calendar.
On your calendar today: no entries in the calendar artifact.
Mail worth checking: Soerface sent an update regarding a new profile option to delete drinks history.
And that's it for your personal morning broadcast.
[mature adult voice, intensely emotional, tender, radiant; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
[very very long pause before speaking; then mature adult voice, most emotional, strong, warm, radiant, deeply encouraging; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
